Before we dive into this module's content, let's orient ourselves to where we are in the process. By now, you should have a reasonably accurate description of a minimum viable audience you seek to serve, initially, an in-depth understanding of that audience's needs and desires expressed in their own words. At least one expression of a value creating idea to share with that audience for initial feedback. If you're not there yet, that's okay. Just keep in mind that this module, and the modules that follow, will be far more valuable if you've completed all of the course work so far. If you haven't done the work to clarify your ideas to validate precisely, it's tempting to take a shotgun approach with your audience to see what sticks. That method likely will slow you down significantly. We recommend that you spend the time now narrowing down your ideas to validate and then proceed through this module and beyond. It's also important to mention that validation requires assets described in build, part one. Validation is much easier and much more effective when we create some minimum viable infrastructure. For example, you may need to set up a landing page to share your ideas to validate. The purpose of that landing page may be to generate conversations with prospects, which would require a calendar, scheduling tool. Or you may validate your initial ideas by generating leads, in which case you would need to set up an email service provider, we use convert kit. Or if you choose to validate your ideas with sales, you'll need appropriate commerce infrastructure. Your specific needs will dictate the appropriate infrastructure. However, minimum viable infrastructure is the goal initially. Build what you need, and only what you absolutely need, to get started. Do not let tech setup create opportunities for procrastination. If you're familiar with the lean startup methodology, you'll recognize a lot of our thinking in this module has been informed by Steve Blank, Eric Reese, and Ash Moria. The meta principles of lean are durable, flexible, and accessible. They can be applied to different contexts easily. They provide a level of scrutiny to our thinking that can all too easily be colored by biases and egos that don't serve us. Note, to be more inclusive, we'll use the plural pronouns, we slash us slash hour, to represent all of us in the context we're teaching in this module. This module shares a framework to test whether a marketing message will resonate with an audience enough to motivate a desired action. That marketing message is an expression of a solution to a problem or meaningful desire explained in validate part two. Message to market match rarely goes according to plan at the gate. Knowing that the framework we use systematizes learning. Each time we interact with an audience, we get meaningful feedback that improves future iterations. Validate part three and build part one are a flywheel within the larger TDB flywheel. Each turn is another iteration towards an offer that works. What we call a mafia offer, one that's too good to be refused in testing and iterating towards an offer that works, we can use two types of test, low hurdle and high hurdle. A low hurdle test is collecting an email, a free webinar registration, filling out a calendar, an appointment for a call or a fake by button, more about this later, etc. A high hurdle test is collecting money, generating a sale. The two can also be combined. For example, a low hurdle email sign up, followed by a high hurdle purchase right on the thank you page. Broadly speaking, we believe adding friction to the process is better than removing friction. But, as with everything, it depends. Context matters. The most essential data points are meaningful signals. Not all metrics are created equal. A free email sign up may seem like progress, but this could be a red herring in reality. We've seen our share of email lists that have thousands of subscribers but can't, for love nor money, generate a $100 sale. Again, context matters. Presenting a cold prospect with a $100 offer right out the gate could be challenging to test because the level of friction is high. It'll always be easier to ask for an email address than for $100. So, in this context, using an email sign up could be a better proxy to use as a signal to tweak our message to market match until we've hit a threshold we're happy with, at which point we replace the email sign up with a $100 offer. There is no right or wrong way to test your messaging, and we're not going to prescribe any. Use the framework to inform what's best for your unique use case. Let's look at our offer testing framework. Define a clear, falsifiable hypothesis. Define success precisely. Define a timeframe. Build a landing page experiment. Measure. Learn to inform next decisions about whether to change strategy, pivot, or to iterate further down the same path. Repeat, keep moving around the flywheel until you have an offer that is predictably working for the right audience. Will unpack each of the points in turn. Define a clear, falsifiable hypothesis. Defining a falsifiable hypothesis means that we're stating, in advance, what we expect to happen. And we're doing that in a way that can be disproven as well as proven. For example, I expect a 20% or better opt-in rate after sending 200 visitors to landing page X is a clear, falsifiable hypothesis. After sending 200 visitors to landing page X, 40 or more leads are generated or not. There's no room for interpretation and bias. The hypothesis was correct or it wasn't. It's important to note that we make predictions about what we expect to happen before running experiments. Why? This safeguards against hindsight bias, a tendency to believe we were right all along. Once we get past our ego, and we're okay with discovering that a lot of our beliefs about what will happen are wrong, we are free to learn. We express our hypotheses as falsifiable to remove ambiguity from the results. We got the intended result, or we didn't, period. Hypothesis can be a single sentence. But it's important to spend the time to formulate a statement that can be proven, and avoid statements that cannot be disproved. For example, we expect landing page A to do better than landing page B is not a useful hypothesis. Better how specifically? What if A generates more leads, but B generates more sales? Which is better? Our hypothesis doesn't tell us how to make that determination. As Ash Moria says, the goal here is clearly defining the conditions under which a hypothesis can be absolutely proved or disproved, quickly. Speed is key. Otherwise, you simply accumulate just enough evidence to convince yourself that the hypothesis is correct. Define success precisely. We objectively define before running each experiment. It's way too easy to create a narrative after the fact and then believe it. Success can be anything that will move you closer to validating a working offer. Examples include. Number of email signups. Number of webinar registrations. Successful showups to a scheduled call. Money collected, hash of sales or a dollar amount. Number of customers within a defined budget or ROI. Early on, our experiments will likely use low hurdle signals, maybe something like. Success is, 50 email signups within an average CPL not to exceed $3. This creates a clear definition of success where results are binary, either we were successful or weren't. Period. No gray area. Define a timeframe. Experiments need to be timebound, hours, days or weeks, etc., or in some cases, quantity-based, until we spend a defined budget or have collected a predefined number of subscribers or customers. Otherwise, experiments could run on forever. It's important to be realistic. If our success criteria is 50 email signups, but we limit our experiment budget to $5 to 10 per day, it could take weeks to meet this goal. Speed is key, demonstrated by the UDA loop. That said, time is at odds with speed and decisiveness in making a decision, finding a balance between the two is important. Build, measure, learn loop. Points 4 through 7 in our framework embody the build, measure, learn feedback loop. Eric Rees pioneered the idea of build, measure, learn in his book, the Lean Startup. It is a learning and feedback loop for establishing how effective a product, service or idea is, and doing this as quickly and cheaply as possible. In build, part I we unpack the build component using a landing page, or Lean website, to run our messaging and offer experiments. We want to build only what we need to run an experiment effectively, expanding infrastructure over time as our experiments become more sophisticated. Build is always just in time, never just in case. Measure is about objectively identifying and using high signal to noise outcomes in our data and results, because of how we formulate our experiments. We also use tools, Google Tag Manager, Google Analytics, and Facebook's Pixel, to collect data to reveal meaningful signals. Learning happens when we compare our hypotheses to actual, real-world results. We expected a 20% opt-in rate, but the results were 5%. Why? What do we think happened? Or we expected a 20% opt-in rate, but the results were 60%. Again, why? And what do we think happened? Learning is critically important. This is when we decide what to do next to continue moving forward, iterating our way to success. It's also possible, especially early on, that we don't have information available to answer our questions. We may not know why our opt-in rate was only 5%, for example. We might be able to see that visitor quality to our landing page was low, as measured by time on page and bounce rates, but that's just data. We want insights. Uncovering questions we can't answer is a signal that there's more work to do, which may require a different testing approach. Experiment canvases. Using an experiment canvas isn't needed, but can help keep experiments constrained, and make them more fun. Design a better business.Tools has many useful canvases, like this experiment canvas, created by Ash Moria. It's free, but requires an email sign up. Experiment canvas, Ash Moria. WRKSHP.Tools also has an experiment canvas inspired by Ash Moria. It's different than the one above, so pick the one you like the most. Both will get the job done. Your homework for this module is to create your first falsifiable hypothesis to test, design the experiment, specify the time frame, and determine the minimum viable infrastructure to complete that experiment. Share that in the community for feedback. After you've done that, move on to build, part one. That explains how to create the minimum viable infrastructure you'll need for your experiment. Then, up next, we'll add Validate, part four which will explain how to execute your experiment with Facebook traffic. Andrei and Sean.
